title: you look like you love me
awards: CMA Musical Event of the Year 2024
tldr: The career-defining duet. If you only know one Ella Langley song, it's this one — and you probably picked it up at a wedding or a tailgate.
about:
If country radio in 2024 had a defining moment, this was it. A bar-romance duet between Ella Langley and Riley Green that doesn't try to reinvent the format — it just nails every move country has been doing since the 90s, with two voices that sound like they belong on the same porch. The chemistry is the whole song.

What sells it is restraint. Will Bundy keeps the production loose and live-feeling — pedal steel, a clean Telecaster, brushed drums, and a vocal mix that lets you hear them breathe. No big chorus production trick, no fake-rowdy crowd shouts. Just two people who could plausibly be having this exact conversation at a Tuesday-night honky-tonk. The first 90 seconds is basically a meet-cute set to music.

The cultural moment: this is the song that turned Ella from a critic-favorite into a name your aunt knows. Seven weeks at #1 on Country Airplay, a CMA win, an ACM win, and a streaming run that pushed it to #13 on the Hot 100 — almost unheard of for a stone-country duet. At every Dandelion Tour stop, you can predict the loudest crowd moment of the night, and it's this one.

title: weren't for the wind
tldr: The breakout single that signaled what was coming. Quieter than the Riley Green duet, but the song that made Nashville sit up.
about:
Before 'you look like you love me' there was this. A quiet, devastating breakup song co-written with Hailey Whitters that uses weather the way 90s country used trucks — as a stand-in for a feeling that nobody quite has the vocabulary for. The wind in the title isn't metaphorical so much as inevitable: it shows up, it changes things, it leaves.

The arrangement is small on purpose. Acoustic guitar, a high-tuned harmonica, brushed drums, and Ella's voice mixed close enough that you can hear the catch. Will Bundy left a lot of air around the vocal — there's a full second of silence before the second chorus that, on headphones, hits like a held breath.

This is the track that Nashville used to introduce Ella to itself. Industry insiders knew the song before radio did. By the time it was peaking at country, the Riley Green duet was already gathering momentum behind it — but this one set the tone for what an Ella Langley song could be: small in scale, big in intent, with one image at the center doing all the heavy lifting.

title: nicotine
tldr: Album deep cut that streams way above expectations. Honky-tonk piano, country radio's worst nightmare and best friend.
about:
The deep cut that became a streaming monster. 'nicotine' is the rare album track that out-performed its singles on Spotify and TikTok — a slinky, piano-led honky-tonk number about loving someone the way you love a habit you keep telling yourself you'll quit.

The production is the star here. Mickey Raphael-style honky-tonk piano (played by Mark Hill on the record), a Telecaster doing dry comping, walking bass, and Ella's voice low and a little ragged. The whole thing sounds like a 2 AM phone call you regret in real time. Will Bundy mixed it dirty on purpose — there's tape hiss audible in the verses.

It's the song fans flag as the one that made them realize Ella was operating at a different level than the radio singles suggested. The lyric is funny in a Lori McKenna way — small turns of phrase that land harder than they should. At live shows she usually does it slowed down and even smokier than the record, and it absolutely wrecks the room.

title: paint the town blue
tldr: The morning-after companion piece to 'you look like you love me.' Co-written with Riley Green and it shows.
about:
If 'you look like you love me' is the bar at 11 PM, 'paint the town blue' is the parking lot at 2 AM. Co-written with Riley Green and produced by Will Bundy in the same session, it works as the emotional B-side to the duet — same town, same characters, different ending.

The arrangement leans more traditional than its sister song: prominent pedal steel, a slow shuffle, and a melody that wouldn't have been out of place on a 1996 Lee Ann Womack record. The song moves fast — under three minutes — and gets out before it overstays its welcome, which is the right call for a song this melancholy.

It didn't chase the chart heights of the duet, but it's a fan favorite for a reason. When the Dandelion Tour set list shifts into this one, it's usually the moment the crowd goes from singing along to standing still. Quiet songs played to arenas have a particular weight, and this one carries it.

title: choosin' texas
tldr: The record-breaker. First female country triple #1 since Taylor Swift, and the song that cemented her as a generational artist.
about:
The song that broke the ceiling. 'choosin' texas' became the first female country single to simultaneously top the Hot 100, Country Airplay, and Hot Country Songs charts since Taylor Swift in 2012 — a feat country radio purists insisted wasn't possible for a traditional-leaning song in 2026.

The genius of the record is how unflashy it is. Ashley Gorley and Jessie Jo Dillon — two of Nashville's most reliable hit-writers — built a song around the simplest possible premise: she's from Alabama, she's leaving Alabama, and she's not coming back. The instrumentation is patient (acoustic guitar, brushed drums for the first verse, slow string crescendo across the second chorus) and the vocal is mixed unusually dry. There's no big production moment because the song doesn't need one.

Seven weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 is the headline number, but the streaming behavior tells the real story: 'choosin' texas' built its audience over months, not in a single launch week. Country fans found it first, then crossover, then everyone. By April 2026 it was the unofficial soundtrack of the Dandelion Tour's opening montage, and the line about Birmingham was its own moment at every show.

title: don't mind if i do
tldr: The 'you look like you love me' sequel everyone wanted. Same chemistry, more confidence.
about:
The most predictable career move in country music, and somehow it still landed. After 'you look like you love me' became a cultural moment, the question wasn't whether Ella and Riley Green would do another duet — it was whether they could possibly match the first one. They didn't try to. They wrote past it.

'don't mind if i do' is the same two characters two years later, sharper and a little tired. Where the first song was a meet-cute, this one is a knowing reunion — both of them aware of the pattern, both of them showing up anyway. The production picks up the same Telecaster vocabulary as the original but is fuller, with more confident drums and a small brass detail in the bridge that didn't exist in the Hungover-era sound.

It hit #1 on Country Airplay the week of release — the fastest #1 in Riley Green's career and Ella's third. Whether it gets the same long-tail streaming run as the original is the question everyone is watching. So far the answer is: maybe, but for different reasons. This one is the song the fans wanted. The first one was the song nobody expected.

title: never met anyone like you
tldr: Ella + HARDY = rock-country thunder. The hardest song on Dandelion.
about:
The Dandelion track that surprised everyone, including probably HARDY. On paper it shouldn't work: Ella's lane is the porch and the honky-tonk, HARDY's lane is arena rock dressed in flannel. The middle ground is a song where both of them are doing what they do best at full volume.

The production is intentionally heavy. Drums sit forward in the mix, the guitars are tuned down a step, and the chorus has actual distortion pedals — a rare move for a Will Bundy record. Bundy has talked about wanting the song to feel like 'a 2003 Tim McGraw single if Tim McGraw made it in 2026,' and that's basically the result.

It peaked at #6 on Country Airplay (lower than the bigger singles, but radio is conservative about anything that loud) and #22 on the Hot 100, which puts it firmly in the top tier of crossover moments. At live shows it's the song the band uses to wake the crowd up at the back of the set — the heat-check moment before the final ballad-and-encore stretch. It works every time.

title: be her
tldr: The album's emotional peak. Co-written with Lori McKenna and you can hear it.
about:
The song everyone names when they're asked which Dandelion track will still matter in ten years. Co-written with Lori McKenna and Hailey Whitters — two writers who could have made this song heavy and didactic — it instead lands light and specific. It's a song about figuring out who you're allowed to be when nobody's watching.

McKenna's hand is unmistakable in the lyric — small images, ordinary places, a refusal to reach for the dramatic statement. The production is restrained to match: a fingerpicked acoustic, a single piano in the second verse, a violin (Mickey Galyean's only credit on the record) that holds long notes under the chorus. There's no drum kit until the bridge, and even then it's a single floor tom.

It was never released as a single but is the track Ella has talked about most in interviews. It's also the song she's most often asked to sing at industry events — the kind of song that gets covered at songwriter rounds and turns up at the CMA Awards' in-memoriam segments. A working musician's song. Quiet, unshowy, and impossible to forget once you've spent time with it.

title: loving life again
tldr: The Sunday-morning sing-along. Closes Dandelion's first side and starts the show on most tour stops.
about:
The closest thing Dandelion has to a feel-good single. 'loving life again' is a recovery song that refuses to sound like one — no triumphant key change, no big emotional reveal. Just a quiet declaration that the bad stretch is over and the small good days are stacking up.

Aaron Raitiere's fingerprints are on the lyrical economy here: every line does double duty. Ashley Gorley brought the melodic backbone. Bundy's production is sunlit — acoustic-led, with a Wurlitzer that comes in halfway through the first verse and stays for the rest of the song. Backing vocals on the bridge are tracked by Hailey Whitters, uncredited.

It's the song that opens most Dandelion Tour shows in 2026. Ella has talked about choosing it as the show-opener specifically because it sets a tone — quiet confidence, not big-arena spectacle. The crowd reaction at the Ryman taping (released on her YouTube channel) is worth watching: by the second chorus, the entire room is singing it back without prompting.

title: dandelion
tldr: The title track that gave the album and tour their name. The 'why' of this record.
about:
The mission-statement track. Title songs are notoriously hard to pull off — they have to earn the album's name without explaining it — and this one does both. 'dandelion' is a four-minute meditation on small-town resilience built around the simplest possible metaphor: the flower nobody plants and nobody can kill.

Written with Lori McKenna and Jessie Jo Dillon, the song is structured as three verses and two choruses, with no bridge. That's a McKenna move — she's allergic to bridges. The production matches: there's exactly one moment of dynamic lift (the second chorus), and the rest of the song stays at the same warm, mid-low energy. It rewards repeat listens because the small variations between verses do all the storytelling.

For a song that closes a sprawling album, it's surprisingly understated — no orchestra, no choir, no big closing statement. The instinct to go small at the end is one of the things separating Dandelion from a typical 2026 country major-label record. The album closer is supposed to be the album closer. This one earns it by sounding like the same song the rest of the record has been working toward.

title: alabama plates
tldr: The track that opens side B. About the moment you realize the home you left isn't the home you're driving back to.
about:
If 'choosin' texas' is about leaving Alabama, 'alabama plates' is about the road back. Side B of Dandelion opens with this — a mid-tempo driving song that uses license plates the way the title track uses the flower. The plates are the only fixed thing about who she is. Everything else is up for revision.

Hunter Phelps and Jessie Jo Dillon co-write, and the song reflects both of their strengths — Phelps loves a specific physical detail, and Dillon writes choruses you can hear on first listen. Will Bundy's production is the most road-trip of any Dandelion track: a slap-back delay on the rhythm guitar, prominent hi-hat, and a synth pad that's quiet but doing a lot of work in the chorus.

It got a country radio push and topped out at #19 on Country Airplay — a respectable showing for a Side B track, and well above the typical performance for a non-single. Live, it's the song that anchors the middle of the set. Ella's done it as both a full-band arrangement and a stripped acoustic version at smaller shows; both work.

title: two-step lessons
tldr: The pure-joy track. The one that has couples on their feet by the second chorus at every show.
about:
The Dandelion track that exists purely to make people happy. 'two-step lessons' is a 2:54 country two-step in the most literal sense — written to be danced to, structured around the exact tempo couples actually two-step at country bars (roughly 168 BPM).

The writers (Ella, Aaron Raitiere, Lori McKenna) have all talked about wanting one pure-joy song on the record. McKenna has said in interviews that she 'doesn't get to write happy songs very often' and this was her chance. Bundy's production keeps it traditional: fiddle (Stuart Duncan plays on the bridge), pedal steel, and an upright bass that's mixed up unusually loud. There's no synth, no pad, no production trickery — just a band playing in a room.

At every Dandelion Tour show, this is the song where the lights go up and the floor stops standing still. The unwritten rule, by April 2026, is that you find a partner before the second verse. It's the kind of song that ends with the band smiling at each other on the outro, and you can hear it on the studio recording too.

title: drinking with the lights on
tldr: Honky-tonk piano, walking bass, and a vocal that sounds like 2 AM. Pure Hungover-era energy on a polished Dandelion record.
about:
The Dandelion deep cut that sounds most like the Hungover record. 'drinking with the lights on' is the album's most direct honky-tonk moment — piano-led, slow shuffle, and a vocal Ella tracked in one take with no comping.

The specific reference point is George Strait's mid-80s recordings (Bundy has mentioned 'Does Fort Worth Ever Cross Your Mind' in interviews as a touchstone). The piano sits dead center in the mix, the steel guitar plays a continuous fill, and the bass walks in a way modern country bass doesn't usually walk. The whole arrangement is built around making the room feel small.

Ashley Gorley and Hunter Phelps co-wrote, which is an unexpected combination — Gorley usually writes the radio singles, Phelps the album cuts. Putting them together on a deliberate throwback track was a smart call. The lyric is restrained where Gorley songs usually go big, and specific where Phelps usually leaves room. The result is the song fans cite when they want to argue Dandelion is more traditional than its singles let on.

title: ain't sober yet
tldr: Funny without being a joke song. The one that gets the loudest singalong after the second beer.
about:
The Dandelion track that's funny without trying to be the funny song. 'ain't sober yet' is a breakup-recovery song that lets itself be honest about the specific phase between 'I'm fine' and 'I'm actually fine,' and most of the humor comes from how clear-eyed it is about it.

Hailey Whitters' co-writing voice is everywhere on this one — short specific images, no big metaphors, a refusal to feel sorry for the narrator while also not letting her off the hook. The production matches the tone: a Wurlitzer that's just slightly out of tune, brushed drums, and a vocal that's mixed unusually present (you can hear lip clicks in the verses, on purpose).

At live shows it's the song that earns the loudest crowd participation after the headline singles. The bridge is the part fans wait for — a couplet that's become a quote-tweet staple. Ella usually steps off the mic and lets the crowd carry it. By the second tour leg in summer 2026 it was outperforming several of the singles for crowd reaction.

title: south of nowhere
tldr: Slow-burn ballad about the places that don't show up on maps. McKenna's fingerprints all over the lyric.
about:
Dandelion's quietest moment. 'south of nowhere' is the slow-burn middle track that fans either skip or play five times in a row — there's not much in between. Co-written with Lori McKenna and Jessie Jo Dillon, it's a song about the kind of places you find by accident and forget the name of by morning.

Musically it's the album's clearest debt to Patty Griffin and early Allison Moorer — fingerpicked acoustic, a single fiddle line that comes in late, and a vocal mixed about as bare as a major-label country record allows. Bundy used no compressor on the vocal chain for this one. He's talked about wanting it to feel like 'the song you sing to yourself in the car.'

There's no chorus in the traditional sense. The closest thing is a four-bar instrumental interlude that returns three times, with the title appearing once near the end of the second verse and once in the outro. It's a structural risk that pays off — the song's lack of a hook is what makes the lyric land. It's also why it'll never be a single, and why people will be playing it on repeat for the next decade anyway.

title: let me ruin it
tldr: The most clear-eyed song on the album. About loving someone enough to know you're the wrong person for them.
about:
The Dandelion song that asks the hardest question: what if the loving thing to do is leave? 'let me ruin it' is co-written with Riley Green and Hunter Phelps, and it's the rare country song where the narrator is the unreliable one — and knows it.

The lyric works because it doesn't moralize. There's no 'and that's why I had to go' beat. It's a song about recognizing your own pattern in real time and not being able to interrupt it. Ella has called it 'the hardest thing on the record to sing' in interviews, and you can hear why — the choruses build but don't release.

Production-wise, Bundy uses two acoustic guitars (one capoed up four), a quiet electric playing single-note lines, and absolutely no drums until the bridge. When the kick finally enters, it's a single hit that fans always notice. The arrangement is deliberately uncomfortable — moments where you expect the song to lift and it doesn't. By the third listen it becomes obvious that's the point.

title: your name in my truck
tldr: Old-school country storytelling. Steel guitar, a single take on the vocal, and the line everyone quotes on Twitter.
about:
The Dandelion track that sounds most like a 1992 George Strait record, by design. 'your name in my truck' is a two-and-a-half minute traditional-country narrative — verse, chorus, verse, chorus, half-bridge, last chorus — about finding the name a previous owner of an old truck carved into the dashboard.

Co-written by Ashley Gorley (Nashville's most prolific hit-maker) and Aaron Raitiere (Nashville's most prolific deep-cut writer), it has Raitiere's specificity and Gorley's hookiness. The chorus line gets quoted in screenshots every other week on country-music Twitter. Bundy's production is dead-on classic: pedal steel, fiddle (Stuart Duncan again), shuffle drums, no electric guitar at all. The vocal is one take.

It's the shortest song on the record (2:47) and probably the most beloved by the segment of Ella's audience that came in through traditional country. At live shows it's the moment the older folks in the crowd lean in and the younger ones start filming. The kind of song that won't chart but will be in someone's wedding rehearsal dinner for the next twenty years.

title: i can't love you anymore
tldr: Ella and Morgan Wallen trade verses on a breakup — the duet that landed while she was out opening his stadium run.
about:
This is the one everybody asked about the second it dropped. "I Can't Love You Anymore" is a duet with Morgan Wallen, released April 24, 2026 as part of the reissue of Ella's second album, Dandelion. Two voices on a song about the moment you finally admit the feeling is gone — her ache against his rasp.

The timing is what makes it more than a one-off. Ella spends the summer of 2026 opening Wallen's stadium tour, so a Langley-Wallen duet isn't a random studio pairing — it's the recorded version of a partnership fans are already watching play out live. If you found this site through a "who is opening for Morgan Wallen" search, this is the song that connects the two of them on tape.

We don't have official writer or production credits confirmed for it yet, so we're not going to guess them — when they're published, they'll go here. What's certain is that it's a real, released Ella Langley track, and it's the rare Wallen-adjacent thing that doesn't expire when the tour ends.

title: here for the party
tldr: Ella and Gretchen Wilson cut a studio duet of Wilson's 2004 rowdy-girl classic, straight off their CMA Fest moment.
about:
Some collaborations you have to explain. This one you just have to hear. "Here for the Party" is a Gretchen Wilson x Ella Langley studio duet of Wilson's 2004 signature hit, released June 26, 2026 — right after the two of them sang it together onstage at CMA Fest (the performance aired on ABC on June 25, 2026).

It's a cover, not an original, and that's the whole point: Gretchen Wilson's "redneck woman" era is the exact lineage Ella comes out of, so putting them on the same track is less a crossover than a handoff. The credits below are the original songwriters — Wilson, John Rich and Big Kenny — because it's their song; Ella and Gretchen are the voices on this version.

If you're new to Ella, this is a fun front door: a rowdy, familiar anthem sung by the artist who wrote it and the one carrying the torch. From here, the Dandelion album is where her own writing takes over.
